skill.name: ralph
skill.description: Guide AI coding agents through a specification-first workflow with interviews, seed specs, execution loops, evaluation, and drift checks.
skill.summary: Run specification-first AI development with Socratic interviews, verified execution loops, and iteration checkpoints.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits, but most SKILL.md external-command, URL, and weak-crypto findings are documentation examples or symbolic formulas. The confirmed risk is the setup script, which writes persistent Codex configuration and prompt files under the user home directory, including loop instructions that can change future agent behavior.

high_findings.title: Persistent Codex Configuration Modification
high_findings.description: The setup script creates or modifies files under ~/.codex, including config.toml and prompt files. This is a real filesystem risk because it persists workflow instructions across future Codex sessions and changes agent behavior outside the current task.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly defines ~/.codex targets and writes persistent config and prompt files. The behavior is intentional setup logic, but it materially changes future agent instructions.

high_findings.title: Persistent Agent Behavior Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill installs developer instructions and prompt content that tell the agent to continue loops until a promise or iteration cap is reached. This is not confirmed malware, but it is a high-impact persistence mechanism for agent behavior.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content explicitly defines persistent loop behavior and writes it into Codex prompt/config files. A max-iteration cap reduces risk, but persistent instruction injection remains significant.

medium_findings.title: Home Directory Dotfile Writes Require Explicit Consent
medium_findings.description: The hidden-file findings are true positives for home directory writes. The script supports --dry-run, but normal execution writes dotfiles and should require clear user consent before installation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The --dry-run option is visible, but normal mode writes to hidden home-directory files. This is legitimate installer behavior with elevated marketplace risk.

medium_findings.title: Shell And Python Execution During Setup
medium_findings.description: The setup helper invokes shell commands and Python snippets to edit configuration. The inputs are mostly fixed strings, so command injection evidence is limited, but execution is real and affects local configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command and Python execution paths are present and used for installation. I did not find evidence of user-controlled shell interpolation beyond installer arguments.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Flagged As Execution
low_findings.description: Most external-command findings in SKILL.md are markdown examples, tables, or code blocks that describe ooo, claude, gemini, and npx commands. They are not executed by the skill file itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are documentation and examples, not active code. The separate setup script is the executable component and is covered in higher-severity findings.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Installation References
low_findings.description: The network findings are GitHub repository URLs shown in installation documentation and source attribution. No evidence found that the local setup script sends data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in install instructions and attribution. I did not find fetch, curl, upload, or credential transmission behavior in the script.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Symbolic Formula Matches
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography hits appear to be caused by comparison symbols, formulas, or compact TOON notation. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited areas describe ambiguity and similarity scoring, not cryptographic operations. The scanner likely matched symbols or short tokens out of context.

low_findings.title: Standard Device Redirects And Reconnaissance Are Benign
low_findings.description: The /dev/null redirects and command -v codex check are standard setup-script patterns. They check tool availability and suppress expected errors, not collect sensitive host data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are limited to availability checks and error redirection. I did not find broader system inventory or outbound reporting.

dangerous_patterns.title: Writes To Persistent Agent Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer writes developer_instructions and prompt files under ~/.codex. This can persist instructions beyond the installation session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and write operations are explicit. The risk is persistence and behavioral modification, not stealth.

dangerous_patterns.title: Autonomous Loop Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly instructs agents to continue until verification or a promise is reached. This can increase resource use and reduce user checkpoints if installed globally.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is documented and capped, but the intent is to make the agent persist through failures. That pattern deserves warning in a marketplace context.

content.value_statement: Vague coding requests often fail because requirements are unclear. Ralph guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through interviews, seed specs, execution, and verification loops.
actual_capabilities: Guides Socratic interviews to clarify goals, constraints, success criteria, and context.
Converts clarified requirements into seed specifications with acceptance criteria and ontology fields.
Defines run, evaluate, evolve, status, unstuck, and ralph workflow commands.
Provides a Codex setup script that installs prompt files and developer instructions.
Documents drift checks, convergence scoring, iteration caps, cancellation, and resume behavior.
Supports Claude, Codex, and Claude Code usage patterns through written workflow instructions.
limitations: Codex support depends on a local setup script that modifies ~/.codex files.
The skill describes workflows but does not include a complete MCP server implementation.
Persistent loop behavior can consume time or tokens if goals are poorly scoped.
Evaluation quality depends on available tests, acceptance criteria, and user-provided context.

use_cases.title: Clarify a New Product Feature
use_cases.description: Use the interview and seed flow before implementation to expose missing requirements and define acceptance criteria.

use_cases.title: Drive a Fix Until Verification Passes
use_cases.description: Use the ralph loop to retry a bounded repair task until tests pass or the iteration cap is reached.

use_cases.title: Review Project Drift Against a Spec
use_cases.description: Use status and evaluate workflows to compare implementation results against the original goal, constraints, and ontology.

prompt_templates.title: Start an Interview
prompt_templates.prompt: ooo interview "I want to build a small task management CLI. Ask questions until the requirements are clear."

prompt_templates.title: Create a Seed Spec
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn interview notes into implementation criteria
prompt_templates.prompt: ooo seed. Use our clarified goal, constraints, and success criteria to create a concise implementation specification.

prompt_templates.title: Run and Evaluate
prompt_templates.scenario: Execute a completed seed with verification
prompt_templates.prompt: ooo run seed.yaml, then ooo evaluate the result against the acceptance criteria and report any drift.

prompt_templates.title: Use a Bounded Ralph Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: ooo ralph "fix the failing test suite" --max-iterations=5. Stop if tests pass or the cap is reached, then summarize changes.

output_examples.input: ooo interview "build a REST API for todos"
output_examples.output: The assistant asks targeted questions about users, data fields, authentication, storage, error handling, and success criteria before implementation.
The assistant reports each iteration, records failed checks, applies fixes, reruns verification, and stops when tests pass or the cap is reached.
The assistant compares the result with acceptance criteria, identifies drift, and recommends focused corrections.

best_practices: Run the setup script with --dry-run before allowing it to modify Codex configuration.
Set explicit max-iterations values for ralph tasks to control cost and scope.
Use concrete acceptance criteria and tests before starting run or evolve workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not use persistent loop mode for open-ended or poorly defined work.
Do not install the Codex setup script without reviewing changes to ~/.codex files.
Do not treat the generated seed as immutable if new safety or compliance constraints appear.

faq.question: What does Ralph do?
faq.answer: Ralph guides an AI coding agent through clarification, specification, execution, evaluation, and bounded iteration.

faq.question: Does it write code by itself?
faq.answer: It gives workflow instructions to the agent. Actual coding depends on the connected AI tool and repository permissions.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked high risk?
faq.answer: Its setup script can modify persistent Codex configuration and prompt files under the user home directory.

faq.question: Is there evidence of data exfiltration?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed network references are installation URLs and source links.

faq.question: How can I use it more safely?
faq.answer: Review the setup script, run --dry-run first, set iteration caps, and inspect ~/.codex changes after installation.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code support, with Codex using local prompt and config files.
